primary_diagnosis.name: Polymyalgia rheumatica (GCA screened negative) — steroid initiation to be supervised

pathophysiology_summary: PMR is an inflammatory disorder of older adults causing bilateral shoulder/hip girdle stiffness and pain with prolonged morning stiffness, systemic fatigue and raised inflammatory markers, sparing distal joints. It responds dramatically to low-dose corticosteroids. The acute immobility mimics stroke, cord compression and radiculopathy, and — critically — PMR overlaps with Giant Cell Arteritis, a vision-threatening large-vessel vasculitis. Reassuring features are a negative GCA screen (no headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication or visual change), no focal neurology or sphincter change, and symmetric girdle involvement with distal sparing. Telehealth cannot examine the girdles/neurology, palpate temporal arteries, or obtain ESR/CRP, so steroids should be started under prompt in-person review with bloods rather than blindly over chat; any GCA feature makes it an emergency for urgent high-dose steroids/ED. Fall-safety and carer support are essential for this dependent older adult.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral girdle stiffness with distal sparing in an older adult.
PMR overlaps with vision-threatening GCA; screen cranial features.
Sudden immobility demands a neurological red-flag screen.
Negative GCA screen, no focal neurology, symmetric girdle stiffness with distal sparing.
Lower probability but must remain safety-netted as vision-threatening.
No focal deficit or sphincter change.
Working telehealth diagnosis: likely PMR; arrange urgent in-person review with ESR/CRP and supervised steroid initiation, with GCA safety-netting and fall/carer support.

red_flags.description: New severe headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, or any visual change (GCA)
red_flags.clinical_significance: GCA can cause irreversible blindness; these features mandate urgent high-dose steroids and emergency assessment.

red_flags.description: Focal weakness/facial droop, sensory loss, or bladder/bowel change (stroke/cord)
Immobility with fall/dehydration/pressure-injury risk in a dependent older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: Bedbound dependence risks falls, dehydration and pressure injury overnight.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not actively screening/safety-netting Giant Cell Arteritis, which is vision-threatening.
Starting steroids over chat without an exam or ESR/CRP; also risks masking an alternative diagnosis.
Not addressing fall/dehydration/pressure-injury risk and carer support.
Skipping the neurological red-flag screen for sudden immobility.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Negative cranial ischaemic screen; ESR/CRP pending
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports PMR with supervised steroid initiation and GCA safety-netting.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen GCA and neurological red flags; arrange urgent in-person review with ESR/CRP and supervised steroid initiation; organise fall-safety and carer support; escalate to same-day ED for any GCA feature or focal neurology.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Starting steroids blindly could mask GCA/alternatives; missing the GCA screen risks preventable blindness.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: PMR is treated with low-dose glucocorticoids after clinical assessment and inflammatory markers; suspected GCA requires immediate high-dose steroids to prevent vision loss.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on giant cell arteritis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Any cranial ischaemic feature in a patient with PMR warrants emergency assessment and urgent steroids.
